verses.text: Comme lors du premier recensement, Moïse et Éléazar (qui avait remplacé Aaron) ont donné des instructions aux chefs de tribus sur le processus de tabulation.

Après près de quarante ans, Israël a de nouveau recensé ses hommes en âge de servir (cf. chapitres **1–4** ) alors qu'ils campaient dans les plaines de Moab en face de Jéricho. La population totale n'avait que légèrement changé, avec 603 550 lors du premier recensement et 601 730 lors du second. Ce remplacement presque complet de la population ( **26.63-65** ) montrait que le Seigneur était fidèle à Israël en dépit de ses rébellions répétées.\n\n

26:12 The sons of Simeon—It is supposed that this tribe had been pre-eminent in the guilt of Baal-peor and had consequently been greatly reduced in numbers. Thus God's justice and holiness, as well as His truth and faithfulness, were strikingly displayed: His justice and holiness in the sweeping judgments that reduced the ranks of some tribes; and His truth and faithfulness in the extraordinary increase of others so that the posterity of Israel continued a numerous people.

26:13 Of Zerah, the family of the Zarhites: of Shaul, the family of the Shaulites: Final clans of Simeon at the second-year census (26:12-14). The tribe's total (22,200) had shrunk dramatically from the first census (59,300 in Num 1:23), reflecting the harsh consequences of the Baal-Peor incident in which Simeon featured prominently (cp. Num 25:14).

26:14 These are the families of the Simeonites, twenty and two thousand and two hundred: The diminished Simeonite count — the smallest tribal total at this census. The dramatic shrinkage signals the lethal effect of plague and judgment on Simeon in particular; Jacob's deathbed prophecy of Simeon's scattering (Gen 49:7) begins to be realized.

26:15 The sons of Joseph, after their families, were Manasseh and Ephraim. Manasseh is here mentioned first, though Ephraim was preferred to him by Jacob, and the standard belonged to him; not because he was the firstborn, but because he had now the greater increase, though he had but one son, Machir, of whom was the family of the Machirites, and a grandson, whose name was Gilead, from whom was the family of the Gileadites, and who had six sons; of whom were the families of the Jeezerite, Halekite, Asrielite, Shechemite, Shemidaite, and Hepherite.

26:16 Of Ozni, the family of the Oznites: of Eri, the family of the Erites: Continuing the Gadite clans (26:15-17). The Gadites' total — 40,500 (26:18) — represents a slight reduction from the first census (45,650 in Num 1:25), more stable than Simeon but still touched by the wilderness years.

26:17 Of Arod, the family of the Arodites: of Areli, the family of the Arelites: The closing two Gadite clans. The careful enumeration of seven Gadite sub-families establishes the territorial claims that will divide Gad's portion east of Jordan after Sihon's defeat (cp. Num 32; Josh 13:24-28).

26:18 These are the families of the children of Gad according to those that were numbered of them, forty thousand and five hundred: Gad's total at the second census. The decrease of about 5,000 since the first census (Num 1:25) reflects ordinary attrition more than judgment — Gad came through the wilderness years with relatively stable numbers.

26:19 The sons of Benjamin, after their families,.... This tribe was numbered next to Ephraim, being under his standard. Five of the sons of Benjamin are omitted, perhaps because they died without issue, and so no families sprang from them; the names of the five sons mentioned vary a little from the names of them in Gen 46:21 instead of Ehi, it is here Ahiram; and instead of Huppim and Muppim, it is here Shupham and Hupham; from these last five were the families of the Belaite, Ashbelite, Ahiramite, Shuphamite, and Huphamite;

26:20 The sons of Judah after their families were; of Shelah, the family of the Shelanites: Judah's three major clans (26:20-22) — Shelah, Pharez, Zerah. Pharez's descendants will subdivide further (26:21) because his line carries the royal-Davidic-messianic future (cp. Ruth 4:18-22; Matt 1:3).

26:21 The sons of Pharez were; of Hezron, the family of the Hezronites: of Hamul, the family of the Hamulites: Pharez's two sons — Hezron and Hamul — found sub-families. Hezron will be ancestor to David through Ram (cp. Ruth 4:18-22; Matt 1:3-6). The genealogical detail at Numbers 26 prepares the way for the monarchy and the Messiah.

26:22 These are the families of Judah according to those that were numbered of them, threescore and sixteen thousand and five hundred: Judah's total — 76,500 — is the largest of any tribe at the second census. The growth from the first census (74,600 in Num 1:27) anticipates Judah's preeminence under David and the messianic promise (cp. Gen 49:10).

26:23 Of the children of Asher, after their families,.... This tribe was numbered next to Dan, because it was under his standard; one of his sons is omitted, very probably dying childless, and so had no family; from the rest sprang the families of the Iimnite, Jesuite, and Benite; and, from the latter, two others, the Heberite and Malchielite; and it is remarked that Asher had a daughter named Serah, and who also is particularly mentioned as a sister of Asher's sons in Gen 46:17 no doubt but she was a remarkable woman, either for religion, or for wisdom and prudence, or some amiable virtue or grace or another, that she is so particularly taken notice of: according to Maimonides (p) she was an heiress;

26:24 Of Jashub, the family of the Jashubites: of Shimron, the family of the Shimronites: Two of Issachar's four clans (26:23-24). Issachar's total — 64,300 (26:25) — represents an increase from the first census (54,400 in Num 1:29), one of the few tribes that grew through the wilderness years.

26:25 These are the families of Issachar according to those that were numbered of them, threescore and four thousand and three hundred: Issachar's healthy total. Jacob had described Issachar as a strong donkey couching between two burdens (Gen 49:14-15); the tribe's continued growth in spite of the wilderness reflects that quiet strength.

26:26 Of the sons of Naphtali, after their families,.... This tribe, which is the last of them, was numbered next to Asher, because it was with that under the standard of Dan; it had four families in it, the Jahzeelite, Gunite, Jezerite, and Shillemite, and its number was 45,400, being less by 8000 than it was when first numbered. Of the sons of Naphtali, after their families,.... This tribe, which is the last of them, was numbered next to Asher, because it was with that under the standard of Dan;

26:27 These are the families of the Zebulunites according to those that were numbered of them, threescore thousand and five hundred: Zebulun's total — 60,500 — also up from the first census (57,400). The northern tribes generally show more stable or increased numbers, perhaps reflecting less exposure to the major judgment events in the wilderness.
26:28 And the Lord spake unto Moses,.... After the sum of the people of Israel had been taken: saying; as follows.

26:29 Of the sons of Manasseh: of Machir, the family of the Machirites: Manasseh's clans descend through Machir, his only son (Gen 50:23). Machir begat Gilead, whose clan name becomes territorial — Gilead the man and Gilead the region east of the Jordan share the same name (cp. Num 32:40).

26:30 These are the sons of Gilead: of Jeezer, the family of the Jeezerites: of Helek, the family of the Helekites: The Gileadite sub-clans, the inheritance of Manasseh east of Jordan. The detailed division here lays groundwork for the eastern allotment that follows the conquest of Sihon and Og.

26:31 Of Asriel, the family of the Asrielites: and of Shechem, the family of the Shechemites: Three more Gileadite clans. *Shechem* — also a major Canaanite city in the central highlands — preserves a name that crosses tribal and territorial boundaries; the personal Shechem here is distinct from the city.

26:32 Of Shemida, the family of the Shemidaites: and of Hepher, the family of the Hepherites: The final two clans of Gilead. Hepher will be remembered as the grandfather of Zelophehad (26:33), whose daughters' inheritance case (Num 27:1-11) sets a major legal precedent in Israelite property law.
Les filles de Selofad ont initié une réforme juridique importante (voir **27.1-11 ** ; **36.1-12** ).

26:34 These are the families of Manasseh, and those that were numbered of them, fifty and two thousand and seven hundred: Manasseh's total — 52,700 — is striking as one of the largest gains since the first census (32,200 in Num 1:34). Jacob's blessing that Manasseh too would become a people (Gen 48:19) finds documented increase in this register.

26:35 Unto these the land shall be divided for an inheritance,.... That were numbered, and to none else: according to the number of their names; and for this end was the number of them now taken, that the land might be equally divided among them; women and minors, or such as were under twenty years of age, had no share in it; and even only those who were at that age at this time; so Jarchi says, it was not divided to any that were less than twenty years of age, even though they came to be full twenty before the division of the land;

26:36 These are the sons of Shuthelah: of Eran, the family of the Eranites: Ephraim's clans through Shuthelah, Becher, and Tahan (26:35). The names will recur in 1 Chr 7:20-21; Ephraim's territorial weight in the central highlands matched the prominent role Ephraim played among the tribes (cp. Hos 4:17; 5:3).

26:37 These are the families of the sons of Ephraim according to those that were numbered of them, thirty and two thousand and five hundred. These are the sons of Joseph after their families: Ephraim's total — 32,500, down from 40,500 (Num 1:33). Together with Manasseh's gain, the two tribes of Joseph form a substantial coalition that will dominate the northern kingdom after the schism.

26:38 To many thou shalt give the more inheritance, and to few thou shalt give the less inheritance,.... To a tribe more numerous, and consisting of more and larger families, a greater part of the land was to be given to them to possess; and to a smaller tribe, and of the fewer families and persons in them, a lesser share of it: this direction is given to Moses, but is not designed for him personally, for he never entered the land, but died before Israel went into it;

26:39 Of Shupham, the family of the Shuphamites: of Hupham, the family of the Huphamites: Two Benjaminite clans (26:38-41). Benjamin's count of 45,600 (26:41) is up from 35,400 (Num 1:37), a major increase reflecting recovery from the catastrophic civil war that nearly extinguished the tribe (cp. Judg 20:46-48).

26:40 The sons of Bela were Ard and Naaman: of Ard, the family of the Ardites: and of Naaman, the family of the Naamites: Sub-clans descending from Bela, Benjamin's firstborn. The careful sub-division testifies to Benjamin's recovery from near-extinction at Gibeah and prepares for the territory the tribe will hold north of Judah, including Jerusalem (cp. Josh 18:11-28).

26:41 These are the sons of Benjamin after their families: and they that were numbered of them were forty and five thousand and six hundred: Benjamin's total — 45,600 — caps the recovery from civil-war-era depletion. The tribe will produce Saul, Jeremiah, Mordecai, and Paul, its small size offset by outsized influence (cp. Phil 3:5; Rom 11:1).

26:42 Notwithstanding, the land shall be divided by lot,.... That the division might appear to be according to the determination and will of God, and not left to the judgment and discretion of the chief magistrate, though bound by the above rule: according to the names of the tribes of their fathers they shall inherit; that is, according as to whatsoever name of a tribe such a part or portion of the land comes up by lot, that shall their inheritance be: Jarchi says, the names of the twelve tribes were written on twelve scrolls of parchment, and twelve borders or limits of land on twelve others, and they were mixed together in an urn, and the prince put his hand into it and took two scrolls;

26:43 All the families of the Shuhamites, according to those that were numbered of them, were threescore and four thousand and four hundred: Dan's lone son Shuham (or Hushim; cp. Gen 46:23) founded the only Danite clan, yet the tribe's total — 64,400 — is the second-largest after Judah. Concentrated lineage produced disproportionate numbers, perhaps reflecting strong inter-marriage within the tribe.

26:44 According to the lot shall the possession thereof be divided,.... It was by the determination of the lot that the land was divided by inheritance, and that was by the Lord, according to Pro 16:33. between many and few; it was so ordered of the Lord by the lot, that the many should have a larger share, and the few a lesser.

26:45 Of the sons of Beriah: of Heber, the family of the Heberites: of Malchiel, the family of the Malchielites: Two grandsons of Asher (Beriah's sons) found sub-clans. Beriah's name — *bera'ah* (in calamity) — preserves a family origin story of crisis, but the line flourished in the wilderness years.

26:46 The name of the daughter of Asher was Sarah: An unusual notice of a daughter in an otherwise male-only register. *Serakh* (variant of Sarah) reappears in 1 Chr 7:30 with her brothers, and Jewish tradition gave her remarkable longevity. The verse preserves the memory that women were not absent from tribal identity.

26:47 These are the families of the sons of Asher according to those that were numbered of them; who were fifty and three thousand and four hundred: Asher's total — 53,400, up from 41,500 (Num 1:41) — represents one of the largest tribal gains since the wilderness began. Jacob's blessing of *rich food* for Asher (Gen 49:20) seems to have nourished a flourishing line.

26:48 And these are they that were numbered of the Levites, after their families,.... And they were numbered not with the rest of the tribes of Israel, but by themselves, as they were at the first numbering of the tribes; the three principal families of which were, the Gershonite, the Kohathite, and Merarite, so called from the three sons of Levi; but all their sons are not mentioned, of the sons of Gershon only Libni, from whom was the family of the Libnites;

26:49 Of Jezer, the family of the Jezerites: of Shillem, the family of the Shillemites: Two of Naphtali's four clans (26:48-49). Naphtali's count (45,400; 26:50) is slightly down from 53,400 (Num 1:43), one of the more typical tribal shrinkages over the wilderness years.

26:50 These are the families of Naphtali according to their families: and they that were numbered of them were forty and five thousand and four hundred: Naphtali's total. The cumulative census (601,730; cp. 26:51) shows the total fighting-age men only marginally smaller than the first count — Israel preserved its broad numbers through judgment, ready for conquest.

Malgré les guerres, les fléaux et les châtiments, Dieu avait été fidèle à Israël. Le nombre de guerriers masculins avait diminué de 1 820 par rapport au premier recensement (cf. **2.32** ), soit une différence de 0,3 pour cent.

Le deuxième recensement, comme le premier, était militaire, mais il a également servi de base à la distribution des terres aux tribus d'Israël en proportion de leur population (cf. **Jos 15–19** ). • Le tirage au sort était utilisé pour rechercher les conseils divins et éviter le favoritisme dans les affaires importantes (par ex. **Nb 27.21 ** ; **1S 10.20 ** ; **14.41-42 ** ; **Pr 18.18 ** ; **Ac 1.26** ).\n\n

La famille de Quéhath a fait l'objet d'une attention particulière car elle appartenait au clan d'Aaron, de Moïse et de Miriam.\n\n

Les Lévites ont été dénombrés séparément (cf. **1.47-49 ** ; **3.14-39** ). • Cf. **3.15** . • Quarante-huit villes ont été attribuées aux Lévites mais ils n'ont reçu de grands territoires selon le processus décrit en **26.52-56** ( **35.1-8 ** ; cf. **18.23-24** ).

Les chiffres du recensement représentent une génération d'Hébreux entièrement nouvelle, car tous ceux qui sont sortis d'Égypte sont morts dans le désert, à l'exception de Caleb et de Josué. Ce dernier a hérité de la position de Moïse en tant que dirigeant d'Israël ( **27.12-23 ** ; voir le profil Josué ), et Caleb a également joué un rôle important plus tard (voir le profil " Caleb" ). **Nombres 26** documente comment Israël est passé d'une génération condamnée par Dieu (ch **14** ) à un peuple ayant un avenir, comme le prédisent les quatre prophéties de Balaam (chapitres **23–24** ).
